content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conduct of Interest

(916) 322 5660        322 5901        322 6441        322 6444

February 1, 1978

78-06-120

Assemblyman Larry Chimbole
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Assemblyman Chimbole:

Thank you for your letter of January 18, 1978, in which you ask whether a real estate course provided to you is reportable under the Political Reform Act. For this course you have received a set of books and have attended two lectures.

The Political Reform Act requires a legislator to report any gift of $25 or more on his Statement of Economic Interest. Section 87200.* The term gift is defined by the Act as "any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." However, the definition excludes "informational material such as books, reports, pamphlets, calendars or periodicals." Section 82028. Under this section, we believe that you do not have to report the receipt of the books. However, we do not believe this exception can be extended to the value of the lectures you attend. Therefore, if the value of the lectures is $25 or more in a calendar year, you must report the receipt of the gift on your Statement of Economic Interest.

Please feel free to call on me if you have any further questions.

Sincerely,

Robert M. Stern
General Counsel

RMS:nc

* All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

7212D

COMMITTEES
RULES
LOCAL GOVERNMENT
GOVERNMENTAL
ORGANIZATION
JOINT COMMITTEES
ELECTIONS AND
CLASSIFICATION
STATE'S ECONOMY

CHAIRMAN
SUBCOMMITTEE ON
HORSERACING

Assembly
California Legislature

LARRY CHIMBOLE
ASSEMBLYMAN, THIRTY-FOURTH DISTRICT

January 18, 1978

Mr.Daniel Lowenstein
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

Early in 1977, the Lumbleau Real Estate School made
available to me a set of books along with an offer to
attend their courses at my leisure. Because of a busy
schedule, I did not take advantage of the offer at that
time.

In January 1978, I started to attend the schools. The
course consists of nine subjects with lectures at selected
times so that the student can attend at his leisure. So
far, I have attended two and have no idea how or what
my schedule of attendance will be.

My basic interest in the course stems from my interest in
legislation affecting the industry. At this time, I have
not made plans to take the State exam.

Please advise me regarding my report responsibilities.

Sincerely,

LARRY CHIMBOLE

LC:pl

sections.question: Whether a real estate course provided to Assemblyman Chimbole, consisting of a set of books and attendance at lectures, is reportable under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: Under Section 82028 of the Political Reform Act, the receipt of books is excluded from the definition of gifts as "informational material." Therefore, the books do not have to be reported. However, the exception for informational material does not extend to the value of the lectures. If the value of the lectures is $25 or more in a calendar year, the receipt of the gift must be reported on the Statement of Economic Interest under Section 87200.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a legislator report the value of free real estate course materials (books) and lecture attendance as gifts under the Political Reform Act's $25 reporting threshold?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Books provided as informational material are not reportable gifts, but the value of lecture attendance must be reported if it totals $25 or more in a calendar year under the Political Reform Act's gift reporting requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (Assemblyman's letter of January 18, 1978) and FPPC response (General Counsel's reply of February 1, 1978). Extraction focuses on the FPPC response portion as the primary document.

If the value of the lectures is $25 or more in a calendar year, the receipt of the gift must be reported on the Statement of Economic Interest under Section 87200.

embedding.summary: FPPC General Counsel Robert M. Stern advises Assemblyman Chimbole that while books provided in a real estate course are excludable as informational material, the value of lecture attendance must be reported as a gift if it reaches $25 or more annually under Political Reform Act Section 87200.
